sections.subtitle: Current Form
sections.text: Everton are in poor form, winless in their last 7 matches and without a clean sheet in the same period. Brighton & Hove Albion, on the other hand, are unbeaten in their last 10 matches, showing strong consistency, often scoring first and seeing both teams score in recent games.

sections.subtitle: Head-to-Head Record
sections.text: In the last two head-to-head matches, Everton managed a narrow 1-0 victory away at Brighton, while Brighton dominated with a 3-0 win at Everton’s ground. Historically, matches between these teams often see both sides scoring, with high card counts.

sections.subtitle: Season Statistics
sections.text: Everton struggle offensively, ranking low in goals scored and big chances created, though they are defensively decent, conceding fewer goals. Brighton show better attacking intent and ball possession, with key players like Jack Hinshelwood in form, contributing goals and assists.

sections.subtitle: Tactical Insights
sections.text: Everton’s defensive setup may aim to frustrate Brighton, focusing on counter-attacks, but their inability to score consistently is a concern. Brighton’s tendency to score first and control possession could give them an edge in breaking down Everton’s defense.

halftimeSections.subtitle: First half summary
halftimeSections.text: The first half at Everton Stadium saw Everton take a narrow 1-0 lead over Brighton & Hove Albion. Iliman Ndiaye's goal in the 23rd minute, a well-taken right-footed strike, proved to be the difference. Despite Brighton dominating possession with a clear edge in ball control, they were unable to convert their opportunities into goals. Everton capitalized on their moment, showing clinical finishing when it mattered most. Brighton, on the other hand, will rue their missed big chances, as they hit the woodwork twice and squandered several promising openings.

halftimeSections.subtitle: Team performance analysis
halftimeSections.text: Everton displayed resilience in defense and efficiency in attack during the first half. Their ability to win duels and aerial battles gave them a physical edge, even with less possession. Key contributions came from players like Ndiaye, who not only scored but also posed a constant threat, and Jack Grealish, whose assist underlined his creative influence. Brighton, despite holding the majority of possession, struggled with their final touch. They created more big chances but failed to convert, with players like Danny Welbeck and Matt O'Riley missing crucial opportunities. Their higher expected goals suggest they should have been level or ahead, but poor finishing let them down.

halftimeSections.subtitle: Attack momentum breakdown
halftimeSections.text: The momentum in the first half fluctuated between the two sides. Everton started strongly, building early pressure with consistent attacking spells in the opening minutes. Brighton responded with a shift in momentum around the midway point of the half, launching several threatening moves and maintaining sustained periods of attack. However, Everton regained control leading up to their goal, with a notable surge in intensity. Towards the end of the half, Brighton pushed hard to equalize, showing a dominant spell in the closing stages, but Everton managed to hold firm.

halftimeSections.subtitle: Key players to watch
halftimeSections.text: For Everton, Iliman Ndiaye stands out as the player to watch in the second half after his goal and overall impact in the first. His ability to find space and test the opposition defense could be crucial. Jack Grealish also remains a key figure with his vision and playmaking ability. On Brighton's side, Danny Welbeck will be eager to make amends for his missed chances, given his high expected goals. Yankuba Minteh’s creativity on the wing, with several key passes, could unlock Everton’s defense if given more support.

halftimeSections.subtitle: Tactical outlook for second half
halftimeSections.text: Everton are likely to focus on defending their lead while looking for counter-attacking opportunities, especially through Ndiaye and Grealish. They may sit deeper to absorb Brighton’s pressure and exploit spaces on the break. Brighton, trailing by a goal, are expected to push forward with greater urgency. They might introduce more attacking substitutes or tweak their formation to capitalize on their possession dominance and create clearer chances in the final third. Their focus will be on improving their finishing to turn expected goals into actual ones.
